Host-microbe mutualism drives urea carbon salvage and acetogenesis during hibernation

Abstract

Hibernation is a wintertime survival strategy employed by certain mammals that, through torpor use, reduces overall energy expenditure and permits long-term fasting. Although fasting resolves the challenge of winter food scarcity, it also removes dietary carbon, a critical biomolecular building block. Here, we demonstrate a process of urea carbon salvage (UCS) in the 13-lined ground squirrel, whereby urea carbon is reclaimed through gut microbial ureolysis and used in reductive acetogenesis, producing acetate -a short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) of major value to the host and its microbial community. We find that urea carbon incorporation into acetate is more efficient during hibernation than the summer active season, and that while both host and gut microbes oxidize acetate for energy throughout the year, t he host's ability to absorb and oxidize acetate is highest during hibernation. Metagenomic data of the gut microbiome indicate retention during hibernation of genes involved in the degradation of gut mucin, an abundant endogenous nutrient. The hydrogen disposal associated with reductive acetogenesis from urea carbon provides a luminal environment that sustains fermentation of mucin oligosaccharides, thereby generating SCFAs and other metabolites usable by both the host and its gut microbes. Our findings introduce UCS as a novel mechanism that enables hibernating squirrels and their gut microbes to exploit two key endogenous nutrient sources -urea and mucins -in the resource-limited hibernation season.

Introduction

Winter is a time of resource limitation for hibernating mammals and their gut microbes. This is especially true for species like the 13-lined ground squirrel ( Ictidomys tridecemlineatus ), which, thanks to their use of torpor, stop eating for approximately six months each year [1]. This effectively solves the animal' s winter food scarcity problem, but it simultaneously deprives both the host and its gut microbes of exogenous nutrients. This can in turn jeopardize the dietary supply of valuable resources that are needed to sustain life throughout hibernation. However, mutualistic relationships exist between hosts and their microbial symbionts that allow the re-use of existing resources to meet this challenge. One example of resource acquisition during fasting is urea nitrogen salvage (UNS), a gut microbe-dependent process that is used by ground squirrels to recycle urea nitrogen back into their tissue protein pools during hibernation [2], when they lack the typical dietary supply of nitrogen. This process, which peaks during late winter after months of fasting and just prior to the squirrel's emergence into the spring mating season , recycles nitrogen to support tissue

Theoretically, the ability to capture this carbon via urea carbon salvage (UCS) would also provide nutritional benefits to the microbiota and potentially to the hibernator host. The existence of UCS has, to our knowledge, never been proposed in the literature, perhaps because most animals do not experience extended periods with no carbon intake where UCS could be beneficial. This is not the case for fasting hibernators, and preliminary results from our previous study [2] suggested that urea carbon is indeed liberated by microbial urea hydrolysis, biologically fixed, and incorporated into organic molecules. Thus, it is possible that both host and microbes benefit from UCS during resource-limited hibernation. Materials & methods

Experimental animals

From a total of 65 13-lined ground squirrels (39 females, 26 males; hereafter called squirrels), 39 were used for the doubly-labelled urea treatment experiments described in [2], 15 were pretreated with oral gavages of  13 C- acetate, and gut contents from 11 others were used for mucin degradation metagenomics analyses. Squirrels were born in a vivarium on the University of Wisconsin-Madison (UW-Madison) campus to wild-caught female squirrels that were

The pregnant squirrels were housed individually at 22°C with a 12:12 h light-dark cycle with ad libitum access to water and rodent chow (Teklad no. 2020X, Envigo, Indianapolis, IN, USA) supplemented with fruit (apples) and sunflower seeds once per week. After birth, pups remained with their mothers for 5 weeks and then moved to individual cages. Following 2 weeks of ad libitum chow and fruit, food was restricted to 12 g chow per day (supplemented with 1 g of sunflower seeds once per week) to prevent excessive weight gain that occurs in captive-born squirrel pups. The consistent diet minimized diet-induced effects on the gut microbiome and the molar ratio of  13 C to  12 C in exhaled CO2. Squirrels were held under these conditions until the day of experiment (Summer group) or transferred to the cold room at the beginning of the hibernation season (Early and Late winter groups).

In mid-September, squirrels in the Winter groups were moved to a 4°C environmental chamber in the UW-Madison Biotron. The chamber was held in constant darkness except for ~5 min/day of dim light to enable activity checks. Food and water were removed after squirrels began regular torpor-arousal cycles, which was 24 -72 h after being moved into the chamber.

The 39 urea treatment squirrels were studied at in one of three time points: Summer (14 squirrels), Early Winter (12 squirrels), and Late Winter (13 squirrels). Each seasonal group was divided into three treatment groups:  13 C, 15 N-urea with antibiotics, 13 C, 15 N-urea without antibiotics, and unlabeled urea without antibiotics. Mean days in hibernation for the Early Winter and Late Winter groups were 36 d (range 31 -41 d) and 127 d (range 109 -135 d), respectively. Mean body masses on experiment days for Summer, Early Winter, and Late Winter groups were 187.1 g (range 165.0 -224.0 g), 155.6 g (range 123.4 -187.2 g), and 134.1 g (range 105.5 -167.0 g), respectively.

The 15 acetate treatment squirrels were divided into four groups: Summer with antibiotics (4 squirrels), Summer without antibiotics (3 squirrels), Late Winter with antibiotics (3 squirrels), and Late Winter without antibiotics (5 squirrels). Mean days in hibernation for the combined Late Winter groups combined was 92 d (range 68 -145 d). Mean body masses on experiment days for Summer and Late Winter were 206.1 g (range 172.6 -241.2 g) and 151.2 g (range 128.3 -174.0 g), respectively.

The 11 squirrels used for metagenomic analysis of mucin degradation genes were divided into two groups: Summer without antibiotics (6 squirrels) and Late Winter without antibiotics (5 squirrels). Mean days in hibernation for the Late Winter group were 135 d (range 127 -144 d). Mean body masses on experiment days for Summer and Late Winter were 183.0 g (range 144.0 -225.0 g) and 132.5 g (range 116.5 -144.6 g)

Animal treatments and stable isotope breath-testing

For the 39 squirrels treated with doubly-labelled urea, the antibiotic treatments to deplete the gut microbiota, the  13 C, 15 N-labeled urea treatments (200 mg urea kg -1  body mass), the breath testing experiments via cavity ring-down spectrometry (CRDS), and the euthanasia protocol were all carried out as described in [2].

The 15 squirrels used to estimate host and microbe acetate oxidation capacity were treated with  13 C-labeled acetate (500 mg kg -1  body mass) via oral gavage following light anesthesia (4% isoflurane-O2 mixture). Acetate oxidation capacity was measured using the CRDS-based breath analysis, after which squirrels were euthanized. Antibiotics were administered as described in [2].

The 11 squirrels used for metagenomic-based estimates of mucin glycan degradation were administered saline via oral gavage following light anesthesia (4% isoflurane-O2 mixture). On the day of sampling, CRDS-based breath analysis was performed, after which they were euthanized. There were no pretreatments for these squirrels.

In all cases, Early and Late Winter experiments were performed on euthermic squirrels following forced arousal.

Tissue harvesting and preparation

Following euthanasia, we collected host tissues (liver, quadriceps) and cecal contents (a proxy for the cecal microbiota) and prepared them for isotope ratio mass spectrometry (IRMS) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR)-based metabolomics analyses as described in [2]. The IRMS and NMR techniques were executed as described in [2]. All  1 H and  13 C spectra were analyzed using the Chenomx software package (Version 8.5; Edmonton, AB, Canada).

Metagenomics

Generation of metagenomic data from cecal contents were performed as previously described [2]. Briefly, genomic DNA was extracted using the Qiagen DNeasy PowerSoil Kit (Qiagen, Hilden, Germany) according to manufacturer's specifications and quantified using a Qubit Fluorometer (Invitrogen, San Diego, CA, USA). Sequencing was performed on an Illumina NovaSeq6000 (Illumina, San Diego, CA, USA) using an S4 Reagent Kit v1.5 at 2x150bp PE. Trimmomatic v0.38 [4] was used to remove sequencing adapters and low-quality reads, while host DNA was filtered using bowtie2 v2.2.2 [5] against the 13-lined ground squirrel genome

For the carbon fixation-related analyses, cecal content samples from three microbiotaintact individuals were randomly chosen for Summer and Late Winter. Translated proteins from the ORF predictions were compared against the Pfam [7] profiles for proteins involved in carbon fixation and mucin degradation, as defined by KEGG using HMMER [8]. Identified sequences were then mapped back to their contig of origin and taxonomically classified using centrifuge [9]. The percentage of target genes ( i.e. , genes for carbon fixation or mucin degradation) in the metagenomes were calculated by dividing the number of identified target genes by the total number of predicted genes. The percentage of target genes classified to different taxa was calculated by dividing the number of target genes classified to a specific taxon by the total number of detected target genes.

For the mucin degradation analysis, we first identified putative enzymes thought to be involved in mucin degradation from the Carbohydrate-Active enZYmes (CAZy) database [10], including glycoside hydrolase (GH) 20 (exo-acting β -N -acetylglucosaminidases, β -N -acetylgalactosamindase and β -6-SO3N -acetylglucosaminidases), GH33 (sialidase), GH84 ( β -N -acetylglucosaminidases), GH85 (Endoβ -N-acetylglucosaminidases), and GH109 ( α -N -acetylgalactosaminidase). We then compared the translated proteins from the predicted ORFs of the metagenomes against the CAZy database using dbCAN [11] and HMMER v3.3 [8].

To determine differences in mucin degradation genes between summer and late winter metagenomes, we first normalized CAZyme mucin degradation genes by calculating reads per kb per genome equivalent (RPKG) [12]. For each ORF, ORF length (kb) and the number of mapped short reads were determined using bowtie2. Next, the number of mapped short reads for each ORF was divided by ORF length and summed in each sample. This sum was then divided by genome equivalents to calculate RPKG. RPKG seasonal differences were tested using Kruskal-Wallis in R [13] ( kruskal.test ) and average genome size and genome equivalents were tested using ANOVA ( aov , stats package in R) to compare between summer and late winter. Overrepresented CAZymes were identified with DESeq2 [14] and normalized counts were transformed using the Hellinger method ( hellinger , labdsv package in R).

Effects of season and microbiota presence on  13 C-incorporation into identifiable metabolites (via NMR) and protein fraction of tissue (via IRMS) were analyzed using 2-way ANOVA (GraphPad Prism v10.3). Breath δ 13 C was plotted as a function of time following labeled urea or acetate treatment (2.5 to 3 h depending on individual), and then area-under-the-curve (AUC) was calculated and expressed relative to each individual's total breath analysis time. The data for urea-treated squirrels originally reported in [2] were expressed here exponentially to eliminate negative values and enable urea-to-acetate conversion efficiency calculations, which were expressed as the  13 Cacetate abundance relative to the exponentially converted breath δ 13 C AUC for the urea-treated squirrels. Seasonal differences in breath δ 13 C AUC for urea-treated squirrels and relative abundances of gut bacterial genes involved in biological carbon fixation (via Wood-Ljungdahl reaction) and mucin degradation were analyzed using 1-way ANOVA with Tukey multiple comparisons test (carbon fixation) and t-test (mucin degradation). Effects of season and microbiota presence on breath δ 13 C AUC for acetate-treated squirrels were analyzed using 2way ANOVA. Non-normally distributed data via Shapiro-Wilk test were log-transformed prior to analysis. In cases where data remained non-normal, non-parametric tests were used. For data presented in whisker plots, the upper and lower bounds of the box represent 75 th  and 25 th percentiles, respectively; the horizontal line within the box represents the median value, and the upper and lower whiskers represent the maximum and minimum values within the data set, respectively. Statistical significance was set at P<0.05 for all comparisons.

Results

Urea carbon ( 13 C) incorporation into host and microbe metabolome and protein

We observed a total of 10, 12 and 15 metabolites enriched in urea-derived  13 C in cecal contents, liver and muscle, respectively (Figs. 1, 2).  The paired PCA-PERMANOVA multivariate analysis revealed that, among the six treatment groups within each compartment,  13 C-enriched metabolite pools varied with season in cecal contents (F=3.0992, R 2 =0.4025, P=0.007) and liver (F=2.7784, R 2 =0.3766, P=0.009), but not in muscle (F=1.2785, R 2 =0.2175, P=0.288) (Fig. 1). However, among all observable metabolites, only cecal content  13 C-acetate was significantly affected by the presence of a microbiota, whereby its concentrations in microbiota-intact squirrels were 2- to 7-fold higher than those of microbiota-depleted squirrels (Fig. 2A; ANOVA P<0.0001; original data were reported in Fig. S6 in [2] and expressed here in different units). This microbiota effect confirmed that the incorporation of urea carbon into acetate molecules

Microbial involvement in  13 C-acetate formation was not observed in liver despite  13 C-acetate concentrations being slightly higher in Late Winter than Summer or Early Winter (Fig. 2B; ANOVA P>0.05), or in muscle where concentrations of  13 C-acetate were below detectable limits (Fig. 2C).

In contrast to our previous observations [2] that urea-derived  15 N is incorporated into host (liver, muscle) and microbiota (cecal contents) protein pools, we found no evidence of microbiota-dependent incorporation of  13 C into protein fractions of the liver, muscle, or microbiota after  13 C, 15 N-urea administration (Fig. S1). The small sample volumes of cecal contents in the microbiota-intact squirrels required pooling all samples into a single aliquot for IRMS analysis, which ultimately precluded statistical testing. Sample volumes for the microbiota-depleted squirrels were also too small for analysis, likely because the protein fraction of the cecal contentss was largely bacterial and thus absent from the microbiota-depleted squirrels. However, we observed a trend for greater incorporation of  13 C into protein fractions of Summer cecal contents compared with Early or Late Winter fractions (Fig. S1).

Seasonal effect on ureolytic activity and urea-to-acetate efficiency

The CRDS-based measurements of breath  13 C reported in [2] for estimation of in vivo gut microbial ureolytic activity were transformed here to express the  13 C values as positive numbers, thus enabling efficiency measurements (see Materials and Methods). This analysis revealed that, as reported in [2], microbial ureolytic activity was higher in Summer than in Early or Late Winter squirrels, which were not different (Fig. 3A; ANOVA P=0.0017).

E ach squirrel's urea -to-acetate efficiency was expressed as the ratio of its cecal ureaderived  13 C-acetate concentration to its total  13 CO2 abundance, represented as its transformed breath  13 C value shown in Fig. 3A. This efficiency measure reflects the amount of liberated urea carbon that is directed towards acetogenesis. We found that urea-to-acetate efficiency is generally higher during hibernation than summer, with peak efficiency in Early Winter (Fig. 3B; ANOVA P=0.0135; t-test P=0.0112 when grouping Early and Late Winter into a single hibernation group).

Analysis of the top ten bacterial taxa from taxonomic classification of W-L genes, as determined by sequence abundance, revealed a seasonal shift from Summer to Late Winter in the bacterial potential for reductive acetogenesis that could include use of urea carbon (Fig. 4B). Most notable was the finding that the greatest percentage of W-L genes in Summer metagenomes (1.74%) belonged to the Bacteroides , whereas over twice that amount (3.63%) was classified to Blautia in Late Winter. The percentage of W-L gene sequences represented in Blautia spp. in Late Winter metagenomes was over 5-fold than in Summer metagenomes.

Capacities for acetate oxidation

An increase in  13 C breath after administration of 13 C, 15 N-urea indicates that oxidation of one or more bioenergetic metabolites by the microbiota, by squirrel cells, or both is a consequence of ureolysis in the gut lumen. Our identification of acetate molecules in the microbial metabolome that contain urea-derived carbon [2] implicated acetate as one of those metabolites. To explore the site of acetate oxidation in the squirrel holobiont, and whether there is a seasonal difference in capacity for acetate oxidation by the microbiota and/or host, we orally gavaged microbiotaintact and -depleted squirrels from Summer and Late Winter with a body mass-specific dose of 13 C-labeled acetate, and then measured their breath  13 C using CRDS. None had been treated with labeled urea. We found that squirrels from all groups exhibited rapid and large increases in breath  13 C (Fig. 5A), indicative of acetate oxidation and subsequent release of  13 CO2 in breath. Squirrels in Late Winter generally exhibited greater breath  13 C responses than squirrels in Summer (Fig. 5B; ANOVA P=0.0003), but there were seasonal differences in how the presence of the gut microbiota influenced the responses. In Summer, breath  13 C as measured by AUC was highest in squirrels with intact microbiotas, whereas in Late Winter, breath  13 C was highest in squirrels with depleted microbiotas (Fig. 5B). Consequently, microbiota-depleted squirrels exhibited both the lowest (Summer) and highest (Late Winter) responses, with a >3-fold difference between the two (Fig. 5B; ANOVA P=0.001). Season also influenced the time required to reach peak breath  13 C, with Summer squirrels reaching peak values early (~30 min post-gavage) relative to Late Winter squirrels (~75 min post-gavage) (Fig. 5A).

The glycan component of these molecules are complex carbohydrates whose degradation releases a variety of smaller oligosaccharides that are fermented anaerobically, producing energy-rich metabolites including SCFAs that can be oxidized for ATP generation, used as signaling molecules, or other purposes.

We initially planned to examine seasonal variation in recycling of mucin carbon from the microbiota back into squirrel tissues using isotope labeling methods similar to those used for urea [2], but were unable to do so due to a lack of commercially available  13 C-labeled mucin oligosaccharides that are resistant to metabolism by mammalian enzymes and could be gavaged into squirrel guts. Therefore, we used metagenomics to test the hypothesis that the seasonal shifts in gut microbiota composition we previously identified [16,17] is associated with a change in the metagenome from Summer to Winter that favors retention of genes capable of degrading mucins. We focused on gene families that encode CAZymes with the capacity to degrade dietary polysaccharides, mucin glycans, or both, and identified 17 that were overrepresented in either Summer or Winter metagenomes. Of the 17, we found five CAZymes that were enriched in Winter vs. Summer, all of which are capable of degrading intestinal mucins (Fig. 6).

Discussion

This study was designed to provide functional context for our observation that, in 13-lined ground squirrels, carbon contained in urea molecules that enter the gut lumen is incorporated into acetate within the microbial metabolome [2]. Here we show that this effect is restricted to acetate among a suite of metabolites we examined and is more efficient during winter fasting than the summer fed season. These and other findings discussed below support the idea that through the symbiotic relationship between hibernators and their gut microbes, urea is repurposed as a dual-use endogenous nutrient source during hibernation, with both its carbon and nitrogen salvaged and used for synthesis of important biomolecules. Moreover, our findings reveal a previously undescribed pathway for acetate production in the squirrel gut that uses CO2 released by ureolytic microbes for hydrogen disposal via reductive acetogenesis. This helps to maintain an efficient fermentative environment for the degradation of mucin glycoproteins, the most abundant endogenous nutrients in the gut in fasted hibernators.

Like UNS, UCS begins with the movement of urea molecules from the blood into the gut lumen, mediated by urea transporters (UT-B), where it is hydrolyzed by ureolytic gut microbes. We recently showed that the abundance of UT-B in the squirrel cecum increases from Summer to Late Winter, suggesting a greater capacity to transport urea into the lumen during the hibernation season [2]. We also showed that ureolytic bacteria are found in the squirrel microbiota and that rates of ureolysis are higher in Summer than in Early or Late Winter, likely due to the reduction in total bacterial abundance that occurs in ground squirrels from summer to winter [15,16].

Following urea hydrolysis, the next step in UCS involves urea carbon incorporation into organic compounds (metabolites), a process carried out by gut microbes. These metabolites would then be available for use by microbes and/or absorption and processing by the host. To investigate this, we screened the metabolomes of the luminal cecal contents (a proxy for the microbiota) and the squirrels' liver and skeletal muscle specifically for  13 C-enriched metabolites which, because our squirrels were treated with  13 C, 15 N-labeled urea, would indicate urea carbon-containing metabolites. Looking broadly across the multivariate data via paired PCA and PERMANOVA analyses, we found no significant effect of gut microbiota presence/absence on the  13 C-metabolomes of these three compartments (Fig. 1), suggesting that at this level of analysis there is minimal incorporation of urea carbon into organic compounds. However, after analyzing individual metabolites, we observed a significant gut microbe-dependent incorporation of urea carbon into acetate (Fig. 2).

Our finding that urea carbon-derived acetogenesis occurs in the gut microbiota of squirrels was notable for several reasons. First, although cecal acetogenesis by the reduction of CO2 with H2 was first reported in 1977 [18] and replicated subsequently in the hindgut of many monogastric species, the carbon source in those studies was not identified and was generally assumed to be oligosaccharides produced by fermentation of dietary carbohydrates. Thus, our study is the first to report incorporation of urea carbon in acetogenic reactions in the hindgut.

Also notable is that the cecal  13 C-acetate concentrations are similar throughout the year despite the decrease in bacterial abundance during hibernation [15,16]. This is coupled with the sustained percentage from summer to winter of bacterial genes that code for enzymes in the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway (Fig. 4). In this pathway, CO2  is reduced by H2 to form acetate and water, thus linking the CO2 produced by urea hydrolysis with acetogenesis. Taken together, these findings suggest that the chronic lack of dietary substrates during hibernation may induce a compensatory response in the squirrel microbiota that enhances metabolic functions and survival in those bacteria that persist during winter fasting. code for carbon fixation and acetogenesis, facilitating the production of bioenergetic substrates such as acetate. This is consistent with the stringent response, whereby bacteria under nutrient stress such as carbon starvation reallocate resources from growth towards the upregulation of survival-related genes until conditions improve [19 -21].

Another example of molecular shifts activated by changing nutrients is the canonical glucose-acetate transition in Escherichia coli , whereby cells excrete acetate while growing on glucose and later absorb and catabolize it for energy when glucose is depleted. This is facilitated by rapid gene expression modifications that suppress biosynthetic pathways and upregulate catabolic ones [22,23]. Here, we posit that a similar situation may exist for the microbiomes of fasting hibernators. When typical carbon sources in the gut such as glucose derived from dietary polysaccharides are depleted over the winter fast, bacteria likely scavenge for other sources [22]. Urea-derived acetogenesis via biological carbon fixation appears to be one such pathway that persists throughout hibernation, possibly enabled by altered expression of carbon fixation and acetogenesis genes in certain bacterial species, despite an overall reduction of bacterial density due to winter fasting [15,16]. In the case of fasted hibernators, the contributions of pathways that recycle endogenous host-derived compounds such as urea and mucins may play significant roles in energy-sparing, thus conserving stored lipids that are the major fuel source during torpor and arousals [24]. Although we detected no seasonal changes in the proportion of genes for carbon fixation (Fig. 4A), transcriptomic or proteomic analyses might reveal that ground squirrel microbiomes upregulate these enzymatic pathways from summer feeding to winter fasting.

Interestingly, more of the urea carbon liberated by microbial ureolysis appears to be used for acetogenesis during hibernation than during the summer active season. Specifically, the ratio of 13 C-acetate to total exhaled  13 CO2 was higher for hibernating squirrels than for Summer squirrels (Fig. 3B). This suggests that less urea carbon is lost as CO2 in breath during winter, when exogenous carbon intake has ceased and when urea carbon, via carbon fixation and subsequent acetogenesis, would most benefit the hibernator holobiont.

Potential benefits of urea carbon-derived acetate for hibernator host and microbiota

We perceive at least four benefits that UCS-mediated acetogenesis may offer hibernating species during winter fasting. First, acetate can serve as a direct energy source for host cells, with intestinal epithelial cells (IECs) being most likely to benefit given their dependence on luminal SCFAs such as butyrate to meet energy needs [25]. Under normal fed conditions, butyrate is the preferred energetic substrate for IEC; however, when butyrate availability is

A similar shift in the nutritional landscape of the cecal lumen occurs during hibernation, where concentrations of all SCFAs, including butyrate concentrations, fall to 3% of Summer levels. Importantly, acetate concentrations remain at 3040% [15,16] and it is therefore possible that UCS-derived acetogenesis contributes to this energy supply for the host, particularly at the level of IECs.

To lend empirical support for acetate's role as an energy source for hibernators and their gut microbes, we orally gavaged  13 C-acetate into Summer and Winter squirrels. We then used CRDS to measure breath δ 13 C, whereby elevated values above baseline indicate oxidation of 13 C-acetate to  13 CO2. Breath δ 13 C rapidly increased following  13 C-acetate gavage in microbiotaintact Summer and Late Winter squirrels, resulting in similar AUC values for the 3 h post-gavage data collection period (Fig. 5). To distinguish host acetate oxidation from potential microbial acetate oxidation, we repeated the experiments on microbiota-depleted squirrels in both seasons. In Summer squirrels, depleting the microbiota halved the breath δ 13 C AUC (suggesting a microbial contribution to acetate oxidation), whereas in Late Winter squirrels it had no effect on breath δ 13 C AUC but produced a higher peak in δ 13 C that was more quickly attained. Overall, the Late Winter microbiota-depleted squirrels had AUC values 3-fold higher than Summer microbiota-depleted squirrels. These results suggest two things about acetate use during hibernation. First, during summer, gut microbes oxidize much of the acetate they produce, whereas during hibernation, a greater proportion of the acetate is absorbed and oxidized by the host. Thus, the host appears to benefit most from the bioenergetic contributions of acetate during hibernation. We suspect that the primary site of oxidation for microbially generated acetate in squirrels, especially during hibernation, is within IECs. This is consistent with the lack of observable  13 C-acetate in liver and muscle, and with the generally deficient energy state of IECs during long periods of food deprivation [27]. Second, the 3fold higher breath δ 13 C responses after  13 C-acetate gavage in Late Winter compared with Summer microbiota-depleted squirrels indicate that the capacity to absorb and oxidize acetate increases during hibernation, when resources are limited and organic compounds such as acetate are especially valuable. Although we have not directly measured rates of intestinal acetate absorption, these results are consistent with our previous work showing higher normalized rates of Na + , glucose, and amino acid absorption by the small intestinal epithelium in hibernating than summer squirrels [28,29]. Similar results were observed for nutrient uptake into brush border membrane vesicles [30], and it is plausible that similar seasonal effects on intestinal acetate absorption via monocarboxylate transporters underlie the results seen here. A second potential role for acetate produced from urea carbon for the hibernating host is its role as a ketogenic substrate. The k etone β -hydroxybutyrate is the preferred fuel source for the heart and brain of ground squirrels during hibernation [32].  Ketogenesis, which takes place primarily in the liver but can occur in other sites including the gut [33,34], is regulated by the rate-limiting enzyme (3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA synthetase 2; HMGCS2). We showed previously that HMGCS2 is expressed in 13-lined ground squirrel intestine and its protein abundance is 2-fold higher during hibernation than summer [35]. This finding is consistent with those from mice and rats whereby short-term fasting elevates both intestinal gene expression of HMGCS2 [36] and the conversion of acetate to ketone bodies in isolated colonocytes [26]. Furthermore, a role for the microbiota in stimulating hepatic ketogenesis in mice under nutrient deprivation was shown when mice with intact gut microbiotas elevated hepatic ketogenesis following a 24 h fast, but the response was reduced in mice lacking a microbiota [37]. Given that levels of fatty acids, which are the most common substrates for ketogenesis, are likely reduced in hibernator IECs due to the lack of dietary lipid intake, acetate may be an effective ketogenic precursor for the gut during hibernation.

A third potential way that UCS-derived acetate benefits the hibernating host is through its effects on secretory IgA (sIgA), the most abundant immunoglobulin in mammals, and one that provides the first line of defense at the epithelial surface. We showed previously that small intestinal expression of sIgA is greater in torpor phases of hibernation compared with summer levels, part of a suite of changes in the intestinal immune system that appears to strengthen anti-microbial defenses and thereby homeostasis of the host with its gut microbiota [38]. More recent studies have implicated microbial-derived acetate, but not butyrate, in playing an essential role in regulation of sIgA production that helps shape commensal microbiota composition and minimize microbial pathogen colonization [39,40]. Thus, maintenance of hostmicrobiota mutualism during winter fasting may require alternative means to generate acetate for immune protection such as UCS-derived acetogenesis.

Finally, gut microbial acetogenesis plays a key role in maintaining an environment conducive to ongoing fermentation. The fermentative breakdown of complex carbohydrates produces H2 which, if not removed or metabolized, will eventually inhibit fermentative reactions by inhibiting the regeneration of the coenzyme NAD +  from NADH, thus slowing the

Acetogenic bacteria help to keep H2 partial pressure at an optimal level by reducing CO2 with H2, producing acetate and H2O. Methanogenesis plays a similar role in many animals (but without formation of a bioenergetic compound like acetate), though there is no evidence for hydrogenotrophic methanogens (Archaea) in the microbiotas of hibernating ground squirrels [15 -17] or bats [42]. Thus, acetogenesis is likely the primary H2 disposal mechanism used in the hindgut of mammalian hibernators. This is useful for the holobiont during hibernation because, while there is a lack of exogenous complex carbohydrates during the winter fast, there is a continuous supply of mucin glycoproteins in the intestinal lumen that, as described in the following section, can serve as endogenous source of fermentable carbohydrates.

Seasonal changes in mucin degradation capacity in the ground squirrel microbiota

Mucins are complex glycoproteins that are secreted from goblet cells in the gut epithelium that, upon hydration in the lumen, form mucus, a complex viscoelastic matrix that protects the epithelium and serves as a habitat for a diverse assemblage of microbes [43]. Mucins also provide nutritional benefits to gut microbes and their hosts [43], which are particularly beneficial during winter fasting. In the absence of dietary (typically plant) glycans, host-derived mucins become the major substrates that support microbial growth and proliferation, and contribute to host energetic needs by producing SCFAs that fuel IECs [44]. Coupled with our previous observation that mucin (MUC2) secretion continues throughout the hibernation season [17], these molecules, like urea, may serve as valuable sources of carbon, nitrogen and energy during the winter fast when exogenous nutrients are absent. Intact mucins are not digestible by mammalian enzymes, but bacteria that express certain glycosyl hydrolases (GHs) can cleave and metabolize glycan linkages, releasing oligosaccharides that are further fermented by the same or other bacteria for energy, and in so doing produce beneficial metabolites for the host [45]. There are two features of the gut ecosystem that must be in place for mucin glycan degradation to proceed during hibernation: a thermally permissive environment for reactions catalyzed by GHs and other enzymes, and a mechanism to dispose of H2 whose accumulation in the lumen slows fermentation. The thermal requirement is met by the ~12-24 h IBA periods when host body temperature is above 30 ⁰ C. The requirement for H2 disposal, which is likely compromised in hibernators by reduced luminal CO2 generation due to the absence of fermentable dietary substrates, can be met by the dual actions of CO2 production by ureolytic bacteria and its subsequent use in hydrogen disposal by acetogens.

We initially planned to examine seasonal variation in recycling of mucin-derived carbon from the microbiota into squirrel tissues using isotope labeling methods similar to those used for urea [2], but were unable to do so due to a lack of commercially available  13 C-labeled mucin oligosaccharides that are resistant to breakdown by mammalian enzymes. We therefore investigated whether the capacity for mucin degradation in squirrel microbiotas changes over the annual hibernation cycle. Our metagenomic analysis suggests an important role for bacteria with mucin degrading capacity during winter fasting, as the five CAZymes gene families that are enriched in Winter compared with Summer microbiomes all have mucin degradation capacity, whereas those enriched in Summer microbiomes include a mix of mucin and non-mucin degraders. This is consistent with our previous findings that several of the bacteria whose relative abundances increase in Winter are known mucin degraders [17,28]. Moreover, all five genes enriched in the Winter microbiomes were classified to at least one member of the phylum Bacteroidota, whose relative abundance in the squirrel microbiota increases over 2-fold from Summer to Winter [16,17]. Noteworthy genera to which winter-enriched gene families are classified include Akkermansia and Alistipes spp . Akkermansia muciniphila , which is the sole member of the phylum Verrucomicrobiota present in the ground squirrel microbiota [15,16], can subsist on mucin alone as its sole carbon and nitrogen source [46]. This property may be responsible for the over 4-fold increase in relative abundance of Verrucomicrobiota during hibernation [16,17]. The genus Alistipes , a member of Bacteroidota and another prominent mucin degrader [45], also increases during hibernation [16,17]. Interestingly, beyond its role as a mucin degrader, Alistipes has the highest percentage of urease genes in the ground squirrel microbiome during hibernation, increasing nearly two-fold in abundance from Summer to Winter [2]. It therefore plays dual roles in processing of host endogenous nutrients with potential benefits to the host and its microbiota.

Conclusion

Collectively, these findings introduce UCS as a novel, mutually beneficial mechanism through which hibernating mammals and their gut microbes meet metabolic demands during prolonged fasting. We propose a process whereby carbon limitation in the gut of fasted hibernators is ameliorated by a sequential set of host-microbe interactions involving first ureolytic and then acetogenic bacteria. This two-step mutualism, initiated by urea hydrolysis, first generates acetate that is avidly absorbed and oxidized by the hibernator host at levels that exceed summer values and may participate in other host functions including immunity and intestinal barrier function. Next, via hydrogen disposal, it helps sustain the gut ecosystem as an efficient

In conjunction with UNS -which enables sustained protein synthesis in host tissues during hibernation [2] -UCS establishes urea as a valuable endogenous nutrient source throughout the winter fast and underscores the integral role that gut microbiota play in hibernation 's metabolic phenotype. Consistent shifts in gut microbial composition observed across various hibernating mammals [47,48] and the finding that UCS also takes place in summer squirrels suggest that urea's dual role in enabling its nitrogen and carbon to be recycled into molecules with nutritional value may be widespread among mammals. Taken together, these insights deepen our understanding of the host -microbe interactions that underlie metabolic resilience and highlight the vital roles gut microbiota play as animals endure extended periods without external nutrient sources.

Looking broadly across the multivariate data via paired PCA and PERMANOVA analyses, we found no significant effect of gut microbiota presence/absence on the 13 C-metabolomes of these three compartments (Fig. 1), suggesting that at this level of analysis there is minimal incorporation of urea carbon into organic compounds.

metadata.preprint_title: Host-microbe mutualism drives urea carbon salvage and acetogenesis during hibernation

pub_metadata.published_journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
pub_metadata.preprint_title: Host-microbe mutualism drives urea carbon salvage and acetogenesis during hibernation
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: Regan, M.; Chiang, E.; Grahn, M.; Tonelli, M.; Assadi-Porter, F. M.; Suen, G.; Carey, H. V.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract:
2.Hibernation is a seasonal survival strategy employed by certain mammals that, through torpor use, reduces overall energy expenditure and permits long-term fasting. Although fasting solves the challenge of winter food scarcity, it also removes dietary carbon, a critical biomolecular building block. Here, we demonstrate a process of urea carbon salvage (UCS) in hibernating 13-lined ground squirrels, whereby urea carbon is reclaimed through gut microbial ureolysis and used in reductive acetogenesis to produce acetate, a short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) of major value to the host and its gut microbiota. We find that urea carbon incorporation into acetate is more efficient during hibernation than the summer active season, and that while both host and gut microbes oxidize acetate for energy supply throughout the year, the hosts ability to absorb and oxidize acetate is highest during hibernation. Metagenomic analysis of the gut microbiome indicates that genes involved in the degradation of gut mucins, an abundant endogenous nutrient, are retained during hibernation. The hydrogen disposal associated with reductive acetogenesis from urea carbon helps facilitate this mucin degradation by providing a luminal environment that sustains fermentation, thereby generating SCFAs and other metabolites usable by both the host and its gut microbes. Our findings introduce UCS as a mechanism that enables hibernating squirrels and their gut microbes to exploit two key endogenous nutrient sources - urea and mucins - in the resource-limited hibernation season.

3. SIGNIFICANCE STATEMENTWhen food becomes scarce during winter, hibernating mammals induce torpor to minimize energy demands and enable monthslong fasting. However, fasting eliminates the intake of essential nutrients such as carbon. We identified a two-step microbial-host interaction in ground squirrels - urea carbon salvage (UCS) - which counters carbon limitation by salvaging carbon from waste urea. Through activities of ureolytic and acetogenic bacteria, urea-derived CO2 is reduced by free hydrogen to form acetate, whose oxidation provides energy for gut microbes and the host. This process also helps maintain a permissive environment for fermentation of other host-derived, energy-dense compounds such as mucins. UCS broadens our understanding of host-microbe mutualism under extreme nutritional constraints and may represent a widespread adaptation among fasting mammals.
